ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated black glutinous rice entry; closest match is glutinous rice (whole grain). Black pigmentation due to anthocyanin content is not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LQ covers glutinous rice raw; no specific black/pigmented glutinous rice sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for black glutinous rice as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of uncooked black glutinous rice is approximately 45 g (about 3 tablespoons dry), which yields roughly 100–120 g of cooked rice after soaking and steaming.

nutrient_source.title: Black Glutinous Rice, Uncooked — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for black glutinous rice, uncooked (cr_id F05090007). Sodium value not reported in HPB record; estimated at 5 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for glutinous rice, raw (FDC ID 168878), which reports 5 mg sodium per 100 g. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100 g and sugar at 0.0 g/100 g from USDA FoodData Central glutinous rice raw entry as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions or sugar for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for black glutinous rice (uncooked) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 86 is assigned based on published GI values for glutinous (waxy) rice varieties in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), which report GI values of 86–98 for glutinous rice due to near-100% amylopectin starch structure. The lower bound of 86 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (86 × 71.09 × 0.45) / 100 = (86 × 31.99) / 100 ≈ 27.5, rounded to 27.4 for a 45 g dry serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) with meaningful potassium (265 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (4.9 g/100 g); anthocyanins in black pigment have vasodilatory properties consistent with DASH diet principles.
hypertension.guidance: Black glutinous rice is suitable for individuals with hypertension when prepared without added salt; however, when used in dessert preparations such as bubur pulut hitam with coconut milk and sugar, the finished dish should be assessed separately as those additions significantly alter the hypertension profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high carbohydrate load (71.1 g/100 g uncooked) and high glycemic index (~86) due to near-100% amylopectin starch structure, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation regardless of fibre content.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black glutinous rice is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high GI (~86) and extreme carbohydrate density; if consumed, portions should be strictly limited to no more than 50–80 g cooked weight and must not be combined with added sugars or coconut milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, meaningful dietary fibre (4.9 g/100 g), and anthocyanin antioxidants that may support endothelial function and reduce LDL oxidation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Black glutinous rice in its plain uncooked form has a favourable cardiovascular profile due to low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and anthocyanin content; however, its high glycaemic load may contribute to triglyceride elevation if consumed in large portions, and dessert preparations with coconut milk substantially increase saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anthocyanins (polyphenol antioxidants from black pigment) provide anti-inflammatory properties; fibre at 4.9 g/100 g supports gut-immune modulation; saturated fat is low at ~0.6 g/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Black glutinous rice may be consumed in moderate portions by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given its anthocyanin anti-inflammatory content, but its high glycaemic load may indirectly promote inflammation through glycaemic-driven pathways; avoid preparing it with coconut cream or palm sugar in large quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black glutinous rice (uncooked) is a whole-grain variety of waxy rice characterised by its deep purple-black colour, which derives from a high concentration of anthocyanin pigments in the outer bran layer. The grain is firm and opaque in its raw state with a subtly sweet, earthy aroma. Unlike regular white rice, black glutinous rice retains its bran and germ layers, giving it a higher dietary fibre content (4.9 g/100 g) and a richer micronutrient profile including iron (2.83 mg/100 g), potassium (265 mg/100 g), and calcium (22 mg/100 g). It is composed almost entirely of amylopectin starch, which gives it its characteristic sticky, glutinous texture when cooked and is responsible for its very high glycemic index (~86). In Southeast Asian cuisine, black glutinous rice is most commonly soaked overnight and then steamed or slow-cooked to produce bubur pulut hitam (black glutinous rice porridge), a popular Singaporean and Malaysian dessert typically served with coconut milk and palm sugar. It is also used in traditional Nyonya kueh and festive preparations. Nutritionally, the uncooked grain provides 350 kcal, 7.46 g protein, 2.87 g fat, and 71.09 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high carbohydrate density and high GI, but its anthocyanin content and low sodium make it a reasonable choice for cardiovascular and hypertension management when prepared plainly and in controlled portions.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F05090007, confidence 0.95 per source record). Sodium and saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central glutinous rice raw entry (FDC ID 168878) as HPB does not disaggregate these values; these secondary fields are marked accordingly. GI assigned as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for glutinous rice varieties.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for black glutinous rice (uncooked) is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific black glutinous rice entry is not reported in the HPB database; USDA FoodData Central reports approximately 12.9 g water per 100 g for raw glutinous rice but this has not been confirmed for the black variety and is therefore not populated. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this entry and have not been populated from secondary sources to avoid mixing source provenance without explicit confirmation. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central glutinous rice raw data; black glutinous rice contains negligible free sugars as carbohydrate is predominantly starch. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as this is an unprocessed whole grain with no industrial trans fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source; plant-based grain foods contain no cholesterol. Description in input references 'burbur cha cha' which is a different dessert from bubur pulut hitam; black glutinous rice is more commonly associated with bubur pulut hitam — this has been corrected in the LLM description.
